sn34.1:1.1: At Sāvatthī. "Bhikkhus, there are these four kinds of meditators. What four? sn.iii.264
sn34.1:1.2:
"Here, bhikkhus, a meditator is skilled in concentration regarding concentration but not skilled in attainment regarding concentration.[n.298] Spk: Skilled in concentration (samādhikusala): skilled in determining the factors thus, "The first jhāna has five factors, the second three factors," etc. But not skilled in attainment regarding concentration (na samādhismiṃ samāpattikusala): though he makes the mind pliant by gladdening it (Spk-pṭ: by removing the opposed states and collecting the cooperative causes), he is unable to attain the jhāna.
Mp III 354, on samāpattikusala: "Having collected suitable food and climate, he is skilled in attaining concentration—adroit, capable, and adept at it."
I doubt these explanations capture the intended meaning and think it more likely skill in attainment is synonymous with the "mastery in attainment" (samāpattivasī) described at Paṭis I 100 thus: "He attains the first jhāna (etc.) where, when, and for as long as he wishes; he has no difficulty in attaining."
sn34.1:1.3: "Here a meditator is skilled in attainment regarding concentration but not skilled in concentration regarding concentration.
sn34.1:1.4: "Here a meditator is skilled neither in concentration regarding concentration nor in attainment regarding concentration.
sn34.1:1.5: "Here a meditator is skilled both in concentration regarding concentration and in attainment regarding concentration.
sn34.1:1.6: "Therein, bhikkhus, the meditator who is skilled both in concentration regarding concentration and in attainment regarding concentration is the chief, the best, the foremost, the highest, the most excellent of these four kinds of meditators.
sn34.1:1.7: "Just as, bhikkhus, from a cow comes milk, from milk comes cream, from cream comes butter, from butter comes ghee, and from ghee comes cream-of-ghee,[n.299] Sappimaṇḍa. See II, n. 64. which is reckoned the best of all these, so too the meditator who is skilled both in concentration regarding concentration and in attainment regarding concentration is the chief, the best, the foremost, the highest, the most excellent of these four kinds of meditators."
